audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Deuteronomy / Devarim Moses-succession, Torah deposit, public reading, and song-commission discourse, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Moses at 120, Joshua's public commissioning, conquest rhetoric, Torah writing and seven-year public reading, inclusion of women/children/sojourners, Divine hiding-face language, song as witness, scroll beside ark, and heavens/earth testimony.

verses.restored: Moses went and spoke these words to all Israel.
He said to them, One hundred twenty years old am I today; I am no longer able to go out and come in, and YHWH said to me, You shall not cross this Jordan.
YHWH your Divine, he is crossing before you; he will destroy these nations from before you, and you shall possess them. Joshua, he is crossing before you, as YHWH spoke.
YHWH will do to them as he did to Sihon and Og, kings of Amorite, and to their land, whom he destroyed.
YHWH will give them before you, and you shall do to them according to all command I commanded you.
Be strong and courageous; do not fear or be terrified before them, for YHWH your Divine, he is the one going with you; he will not fail you or forsake you.

Moses called Joshua and said to him before eyes of all Israel, Be strong and courageous, for you shall go with this people into land YHWH swore to their fathers to give them, and you shall cause them to inherit it.
YHWH, he is the one going before you; he will be with you, he will not fail you or forsake you. Do not fear and do not be shattered.
Moses wrote this Torah/instruction and gave it to priests, sons of Levi, who carry ark of covenant of YHWH, and to all elders of Israel.
Moses commanded them, saying, At end of seven years, at appointed time of year of release, at Festival of Booths,
when all Israel comes to appear before YHWH your Divine at place he chooses, you shall read this Torah before all Israel in their hearing.
Assemble people—men, women, little ones, and your sojourner within your gates—so they may hear and so they may learn and fear/revere YHWH your Divine and keep to do all words of this Torah.
Their children, who have not known, shall hear and learn to fear/revere YHWH your Divine all days you live upon ground where you are crossing Jordan to possess it.

YHWH said to Moses, Look, your days draw near to die. Call Joshua and station yourselves in tent of meeting, and I will commission him. So Moses and Joshua went and stationed themselves in tent of meeting.
YHWH appeared in tent in pillar of cloud, and pillar of cloud stood at entrance of tent.

YHWH said to Moses, Look, you are lying down with your fathers, and this people will rise and prostitute itself after foreign gods of land into whose midst it enters; it will forsake me and break my covenant that I cut with it.

My anger will burn against it on that day, and I will forsake them and hide my face from them, and they shall become food; many evils and troubles shall find them, and they shall say on that day, Is it not because my Divine is not in my midst that these evils found me?
But I will surely hide my face on that day because of all evil it did, for it turned to other gods.
Now write for yourselves this song and teach it to children of Israel; put it in their mouths, so this song may be for me as witness against children of Israel.

For I will bring them to ground I swore to their fathers, flowing with milk and honey, and they will eat and be satisfied and grow fat; then they will turn to other gods and serve them and despise me and break my covenant.

It shall be, when many evils and troubles find them, this song shall answer before them as witness, for it shall not be forgotten from mouth of their seed; for I know their inclination that they are forming today, before I bring them into land I swore.
Moses wrote this song on that day and taught it to children of Israel.
He commissioned Joshua son of Nun and said, Be strong and courageous, for you shall bring children of Israel into land I swore to them, and I will be with you.
It was, when Moses finished writing words of this Torah in a scroll until they were complete,
Moses commanded Levites who carry ark of covenant of YHWH, saying,
Take this scroll of Torah and place it beside ark of covenant of YHWH your Divine, and it shall be there as witness against you.
For I know your rebellion and your stiff neck. Look, while I am still alive with you today you have been rebellious with YHWH; how much more after my death?
Assemble to me all elders of your tribes and your officers, and I will speak in their ears these words and call heavens and earth as witnesses against them.

For I know that after my death you will surely act corruptly and turn aside from way I commanded you; evil will meet you in latter days, because you will do evil in eyes of YHWH, provoking him with work of your hands.
Moses spoke in ears of all assembly of Israel words of this song until they were complete.

translation_notes: Moses' succession is public: Joshua is strengthened before all Israel rather than privately installed through dynasty.
The conquest language continues total-war traditions and must remain under anti-genocide and anti-colonial safeguards.
Every seventh year Torah is read aloud at Sukkot to men, women, children, and immigrants; public access to instruction is a covenant obligation.
The sexual metaphor in verse 16 describes religious betrayal but can reinforce misogynistic language if detached from its rhetorical setting; it must not be used to shame women or sexual minorities.
Growing fat in verse 20 is a prosperity-and-forgetting metaphor, not permission for body shaming.
Hiding-face language articulates perceived abandonment during catastrophe; it must not be used to tell traumatized people that Divine absence caused their suffering.
Stiff-necked language is internal covenant critique and may never support antisemitic characterization.
The Torah scroll is placed beside the ark as a public legal witness, while the song is placed in communal memory and speech.
safety_note: Do not use Deuteronomy 31 to justify genocide, misogynistic sexual metaphors, body shaming, antisemitism, authoritarian succession, or telling sufferers that Divine abandonment explains their trauma. Center public leadership transition, broad access to Torah, intergenerational teaching, courage without domination, and communal memory that outlives one leader.
